APPLICATION DETAILS.Scientific Abstract:
At the pre-doctoral fellowship level, applicants are not expected to have a fully developed PhD proposal (this will be an output of the award); however, candidates are required to identify their area of research interest and the methodologies this research may involve. This is the research area in which they will develop their doctoral fellowship proposal.
Please describe your research area of interest. Applicants should also describe why they wish to develop their career within this research area.
For “Standard” PCAF applicants, the identification of just the research area in which they will be developing their doctoral fellowship proposal is sufficient.
The research area should inform the proposed training plan and the proposed supervisory team and is used by the Selection committee to assess the suitability of the proposed training and supervision.
The research area should align with the applicant’s professional practice. If the applicant’s current professional practice does not align with their area of research interest, the applicant will need to clearly demonstrate how they will work towards such alignment to support a competitive doctoral fellowship application.
It is accepted that, as academic training and research experience progresses, research ideas and interests may change and, if the PCAF is funded, candidates will not be held to this stated area of interest for their doctoral fellowships.
(Word Limit 250)
My research area of interest is improving glaucoma care delivery using a data science approach.
Glaucoma is the world-leading cause of irreversible sight loss, affecting more than 4% of individuals over 50 in the UK.  Treatment follows a trial-and-error process and requires lifelong monitoring, placing significant burden on health services.  Glaucoma services are already the busiest outpatient specialty within the NHS – accounting for 7.5% of outpatient activity across all specialties – and are struggling to cope with demand from an ageing population and backlogs from the Covid-19 pandemic.  22 patients each month are estimated to suffer severe or permanent sight loss from glaucoma due to delayed reviews - this was recognised as a national safety issue and a call for ‘smarter ways of working’ has been made to address this.
Improving the ability to risk-stratify patients and predict optimal treatment is instrumental to this problem.  Approximately 20% of patients in glaucoma clinics are monitored as ‘suspects’ due to difficulty in identifying early disease.  Our group has shown the predictive ability of polygenic risk scores for glaucoma. The linkage of UK Biobank and national electronic medical records data offers exciting potential to use such models to risk stratify and predict response to treatments.  As a clinician delivering glaucoma care, I understand first-hand the pressure the NHS is under and have witnessed the effect of sight loss on patients.  This is why I want to pursue this line of research and help implement changes that improve the health service and patient outcomes.

APPLICATION DETAILS.Working with People and Communities Summ: I will complete the Introduction to Public-Patient Involvement and Introduction to Equality, Diversity and Inclusion in Ophthalmic Research courses (available at no cost).  This prepares me to run a patient focus group to inform the doctoral application development.  A previous focus group revealed that patients strongly support the use of genetic testing to enable early diagnosis and predict best treatment.  I will build on this to garner public opinion on my implementation proposal.  Recruitment will be supported by the PPI team at the NIHR Moorfields Biomedical Research Centre and will aim to recruit the most diverse group possible.

(Word limit: 1000)
My proposed primary supervisor is Professor Prof Khawaja Khawaja, Professor of Ophthalmology at UCL where he leads an internationally reputed data science research group, and Honorary Consultant Ophthalmologist at Moorfields Eye Hospital where he specialises in the care of adults with glaucoma.
Prof Khawaja’s primary research interest is in the genetic and environmental causes of glaucoma, and his group aims to translate discoveries into clinical prediction tools to enable personalised glaucoma care.  His ultimate research aim is to combine polygenic models with machine learning image algorithms to develop predictive clinical tools for glaucoma management, enabling targeted population screening and stratified, individualised care in clinics.  He is currently UKRI Future Leaders Fellow and has been awarded one of the 2022 Lister Institute Research Prizes to support his research programme.
He is a recognised leading investigator in many aspects of data-driven research, including real-world outcomes, exploratory big data studies and statistical methods.  He leads an internationally reputed common eye diseases complex genetics programme and was first author and primary analyst for the largest genome-wide association study (GWAS) of intraocular pressure to date, published in Nature Genetics.  This was described as ‘landmark’ due to the leap forward in glaucoma genetics understanding and generated much media interest (Altmetric 211).  The 112 genetic loci identified predicted a substantial proportion of people with glaucoma in two independent studies, prompting current research examining the effectiveness of population screening targeted to people at high genetic risk.  He also identified biological pathways which point to pathology in parts of the eye previously not thought to be important in glaucoma, opening up the rationale for new treatments (which are already being developed) and helping to sub-categorise glaucoma and tailor treatments accordingly. Prof Khawaja was also joint first author for a landmark GWAS for primary open-angle glaucoma (Nature Communications, 2021) and refractive error (Nature Genetics, 2020).  A recent major impact from his research has been the direct to consumer genotyping company, 23andMe, starting to report a polygenic risk score for glaucoma, and suggesting caffeine reduction to reduce risk.  His extensive knowledge in the subject matter and the relevant methodologies will be invaluable for supporting the development of my doctoral research plan.
He is also leading the world’s largest data resources that combine clinical outcome and genomic data, including leading a large-scale linkage of national ophthalmic data with UK Biobank which has recently commenced.  This is anticipated to be the basis of my Masters and PhD project and he will provide access to this.
Prof Khawaja leads a 3-hour ocular informatics bi-weekly meeting at the UCL Institute of Ophthalmology; researchers within and external to his UCL group present their analyses and receive constructive feedback from the broad expertise that attends (including epidemiology, statistics, computer science, mathematics, genomics). He also hosts journal clubs and external speakers. The group meetings are highly interactive, encourage open discussion of ideas and have a strong track record for high quality research outputs as a result.  I will attend these meetings and receive feedback on my research from this broad range of experts.  Prof Khawaja will be available for regular 1-1 meetings in addition to this.
Prof Khawaja is an accomplished educator, supervisor and mentor with a successful track record of research supervision; having successfully supervised 2 PhD students and 4 Masters students to completion.  He currently supervises 3 PhD students. He is Director of the European Society of Ophthalmology Leadership Development Programme, leading a multi-module programme to enhance leadership skills of emerging ophthalmology leaders from UK/Europe nominated by their national societies. He was also Director of the Academy for Research Training, European School for Advanced Studies in Ophthalmology; he developed and led a multi-module research skills training programme for early-career researchers across Europe. He lectures for the UCL Clinical Ophthalmology MSc and Bioscience Entrepreneurship MSc on Genome-Wide Association Studies, Mendelian Randomisation, Glaucoma Epidemiology and Genetics, Statistical Pitfalls in Ophthalmology and Big Data Research in Ophthalmology (last feedback received: 5/5 from all students across all domains). He has also developed an online interactive glaucoma epidemiology module for the Advanced Clinical Optometry MSc.  Prof Khawaja was Glaucoma Service Training Director at Moorfields Eye Hospital between 2017-2021, responsible for training of residents and fellows and leading the UCL/Moorfields weekly Glaucoma Academic Half-day Meeting. He organised the interactive programme including video-based surgical learning, express-format journal club, wet labs and renowned guest speakers. The teaching was highly regarded and fed back as the "best specialist glaucoma teaching nationally".  My career development will benefit greatly from his established mentorship skills in both research and clinical contexts.
Prof Khawaja is an esteemed researcher with international acclaim.  He holds several research leadership positions, including elected President of the European Eye Epidemiology (E3) Consortium and Chair of both the UK Glaucoma Genetics Consortium and the UK Glaucoma Real-World Data Consortium. He established and leads the successful multidisciplinary international Modifiable Risk Factors for Glaucoma Collaboration, and has a prominent role in the International Glaucoma Genetics Consortium. He is Associate Editor for the journal Eye (Nature Publishing) and is considered a leader in this field, frequently invited as a keynote lecturer nationally and internationally (e.g. 37 invited international lectures in last 3 years).  He is co-chair of the Moorfields-UCL NIHR Biomedical Research Centre; and was a key member of the grant writing group for the £20M award.  He has authored >150 peer-reviewed publications (Citations: 8136, h-index: 45, i10-index: 101) and has won numerous other prizes and awards in his career, including a Bronze National Clinical Excellence Award and a Secretariat Award and Senior Achievement Award from the American Academy of Ophthalmology.  Prof Khawaja has been peer-voted in top 100 thought leaders in ophthalmology globally twice for The Ophthalmologist publication.  Early in the pandemic, he established and chaired a regular global webinar with international guests and expert panellists; there have been >600 unique participants from UK & around the world.  His reputed expertise in the field coupled with his established track-record as a mentor makes him the ideal PhD supervisor if this award is successful.

The individual listed here must also be added as a participant in the ‘Participants and Signatories’ section of the application form.
Dr Dun Jack Fu will be the proposed second supervisor, who is an NIHR Academic Clinical lecturer at the UCL Institute of Ophthalmology and Honorary Clinical Researcher at Moorfields Eye Hospital.
Dr. Fu is trained in molecular biochemistry, high-throughput screening and bioinformatics through his PhD research as a Wellcome Trust Doctoral Fellow at the Centre for Dermatology and Genetic Medicine.  He subsequently completed medical training at University of Oxford and is undertaking ophthalmology specialty training at Moorfields Eye Hospital.  His current post-doctoral research focuses on the development of clinical prediction modelling with large, multimodal datasets using both traditional prediction modelling approaches and modern machine learning techniques.  This expertise is highly pertinent to my research area of interest and will greatly benefit my training.  His experience in conducting data-intensive research makes him well placed to support my doctoral research plan development.
He is also an experienced educator.  He leads the Digital Health & AI module on the UCL Bioscience Entrepreneurship MSc programme and has supervised over 5 Masters students to completion.  He has designed and written content for 2 additional UCL courses – Telemedicine for Ophthalmology Postgraduate Certificate and Data Science for Clinicians.  His insights in developing digital tools for health will be beneficial in shaping my doctoral research output.
As an academic, he has consistently engaged in impactful academic publications and resources, resulting in over 40 peer-reviewed publications - including recent first-author publications in JAMA Ophthalmology and Lancet Digital Health – 2 grants as co-investigator, 3 accredited university courses and an e-learning platform.  These contributions have been recognised internationally, such as Nature Medicine 2019’s most notable advances, Eric Topol’s 2019 list of must-read AI papers, and the EURETINA Prize Paper presentation in artificial intelligence.  His work has led to the appointment as an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellow at King’s College London, followed by his current position.  His successful experiences will be highly beneficial in developing my doctoral application.  He will be able to commit 6 hours a week to supervise my progress and will be readily contactable.
Dr. Fu’s diverse skillset is highly relevant to my area of research interest and complementary to that offered by the primary supervisor.  His successful progression through a clinical academic pathway means he is well-placed to provide additional mentorship to guide my progression beyond this award.  Should this award be successful he would be a highly suitable PhD supervisor for me.

The individual named here must also be added in the ‘Participants and Signatories’ section of the application form.
(Word limit: 250)
Dr Mulholland is the Principal Optometrist (Research) in Moorfields Eye Hospital, Lecturer in Optometry (Ulster University), Honorary Associate Professor (UCL), and an investigator within the NIHR Biomedical Research Centre for Ophthalmology.  He currently leads a research programme developing novel visual function tests for detecting and monitoring ocular and systemic diseases including glaucoma.  He is also clinically active in range of environments.
He has a successful track-record of receiving research funding, totalling £2.3 million to date including a personal fellowship award from the College of Optometrists.  He is active in promoting optometrists in clinical research, representing optometry on the NIHR national ophthalmology speciality group and chairing the optometry subgroup within the UK Eye Research Strategy.
Dr Mulholland is also an established educator, holding Fellowship of the Higher Education Academy and appointed to the advisory committee for the Quality Assurance Agency benchmark statement for optometry.  He has successfully supervised 4 PhD students to on-time completion, with several receiving merit awards such as the George Giles award for best PhD by UK optometrist.  I have already benefitted from his mentorship under which I completed the NIHR Associate Principal Investigator scheme.
His research expertise, track-record of successful grant applications and effective research mentorship, coupled with his unique, concurrent roles within the NHS and academia, makes him ideally placed to offer discipline-specific support to potential challenges.  His extensive connections could also open doors for future collaborative relationships.  Dr Mulholland will commit 3.5 hours each month for mentorship if this award is successful.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM.Host Organisation:
This statement is completed by the Heads of Departments of the named Host and Partner organisations and should detail how these organisations are going to support the applicant, in partnership, to successfully complete the PCAF (if awarded).
This statement should be tailored specifically to the applicant, their research and training needs, and include how the organisations intend to support the applicant to develop their research career in the long-term.
NIHR see the responsibility for training the next generation of research leaders as a joint enterprise with host organisations. Therefore, information should also be provided on the organisations’ track records of supporting early career researchers, such as evidence of bridging or other support provided to fellows upon completion of an award.
In addition, the statement should also describe the host organisation’s approach to creating and maintaining an inclusive and supportive research culture for all. The statement should provide evidence of how the organisation values and supports equality, diversity and inclusion as well as acknowledging the organisation’s responsibilities with respect to research integrity. Statements may wish to refer to the principles and best practice outlined within relevant Charters and Concordats in these areas, such as the Researcher Development Concordat and Advance HE’s Equality Charters. It should be noted that being a signatory to Concordats or holding bronze/silver status from the Equality Charters isn’t a requirement of funding and evidence can be provided through other means.
(Word limit: 1000)
Wing Shing Low has established himself as an exceptional clinician in a range of ophthalmic sub-specialties in the 4 years he has worked in Moorfields Eye Hospital.  Having undergone extensive clinical training, he is now a senior optometrist responsible for specialised care with a high degree of clinical autonomy, as well as supervising staff and trainees in multiple disciplines across the glaucoma and contact lens services.   In addition to delivering teaching and performing audits, he has made significant contributions to two important research projects within the Trust and demonstrated his potential to become an excellent researcher.  Moorfields Eye Hospital and UCL would like to offer our full support in continue to his development as a research scientist & clinician.
We commit to providing 0.9 WTE of protected time for him to develop his research skills.  He will be supported by UCL through the Health Data Science MSc, where he will have access to formal data methods training and the collaborative opportunities within the Institute of Health Informatics.  Further, he will collaborate with the Ocular Informatics Group - led by the proposed primary supervisor Prof Anthony Khawaja – to gain relevant research experience in this area.  The Group is based in the Moorfields Biomedical Research Centre (BRC) - a joint venture between Moorfields and UCL– where he would benefit from the facilities, research infrastructure and collaborative opportunities available there.
Moorfields will also support Wing’s training towards autonomous delivery of YAG laser capsulotomy and glaucoma laser procedures.  Selective Laser Trabeculoplasty is widely used in the management of glaucoma worldwide however there remains an urgent need for further research to expand the evidence base and optimise treatment. He will be allocated an experienced laser practitioner as his clinical supervisor and a dedicated laser list to facilitate training according to the Trust training protocol.  Once complete he will run a mixed laser list to maintain his existing and new laser skills and this will be maintained throughout the award.
We also commit to securing Wing’s position in Moorfields following the completion of this award and will continue to support his future research development.  The optometry department currently employs numerous practitioner academics with a varying mix of research and clinical activity.  It is expected that a suitable contractual arrangement would be found to meet his circumstances.
Moorfields and UCL have a strong track record of collaborating to develop early career researchers..  UCL is a signatory of the Concordat to Support the Career Development of Researchers. The quality of its support systems has been recognised through the Vitae HR Excellence in Research Award in 2022.  The UCL Institute of Ophthalmology have hosted many post-doctoral research fellows from a range of independent funders including the NIHR, UKRI and the Wellcome Trust.  The mentorship and networking opportunities provided through these fellowships have produced numerous research leaders in their fields.  Many of them now hold substantive posts within Moorfields and the BRC who will be available to provide advice and guidance for Wing’s career development.  Moorfields Eye Charity has several funding programmes designed to support early career researchers, including the 12 months Springboard Award and the Advanced Clinical Academic Fellowship co-funded with the NIHR.   Wing will have access to the expertise and advice from a dedicated team at the UCL Institute of Ophthalmology to provide application advice and administrative support for these funding schemes.
UCL considers maintaining the highest standards of research integrity a joint responsibility between UCL and everyone involved in research.  The UCL Code of Conduct for Research reflects the commitments set out by the Concordant to Support Research Integrity and UCL is committed to maintain a supportive environment for researchers to adhere to these principles.  Diversity in research is also fundamental to research integrity and clear strategies to promote equality, diversity and inclusion (EDI) are embedded in all aspects of research activity at Moorfields and UCL.  A dedicated team of co-ordinators and associates continually monitors EDI issues across all aspects of research including funding, recruitment and participation in research at both institutions.  They are also tasked with delivering and implementing solutions to address any inequality issues identified and ensure practice is aligned with principles set out by the relevant bodies.  UCL’s commitment to gender and racial equality is recognised through the Athena Swan Silver Award and the Race Equality Charter Bronze Award.
Awaiting information from UCL about conversion rate of Masters to PhD and progression of practitioner academics along the ICA Pathway

scientific_abstract.text:
At the pre-doctoral fellowship level, applicants are not expected to have a fully developed PhD proposal (this will be an output of the award); however, candidates are required to identify their area of research interest and the methodologies this research may involve. This is the research area in which they will develop their doctoral fellowship proposal.
For “Standard” PCAF applicants, the identification of just the research area in which they will be developing their doctoral fellowship proposal is sufficient.
The research area should inform the proposed training plan and the proposed supervisory team and is used by the Selection committee to assess the suitability of the proposed training and supervision.
The research area should align with the applicant’s professional practice. If the applicant’s current professional practice does not align with their area of research interest, the applicant will need to clearly demonstrate how they will work towards such alignment to support a competitive doctoral fellowship application.
It is accepted that, as academic training and research experience progresses, research ideas and interests may change and, if the PCAF is funded, candidates will not be held to this stated area of interest for their doctoral fellowships.
My research area of interest is improving glaucoma care delivery using a data science approach.
Glaucoma is the world-leading cause of irreversible sight loss, affecting more than 4% of individuals over 50 in the UK. Treatment follows a trial-and-error process and requires lifelong monitoring, placing significant burden on health services. Glaucoma services are already the busiest outpatient specialty within the NHS – accounting for 7.5% of outpatient activity across all specialties – and are struggling to cope with demand from an ageing population and backlogs from the Covid-19 pandemic. 22 patients each month are estimated to suffer severe or permanent sight loss from glaucoma due to delayed reviews - this was recognised as a national safety issue and a call for ‘smarter ways of working’ has been made to address this.
Improving the ability to risk-stratify patients and predict optimal treatment is instrumental to this problem. Approximately 20% of patients in glaucoma clinics are monitored as ‘suspects’ due to difficulty in identifying early disease. Our group has shown the predictive ability of polygenic risk scores for glaucoma. The linkage of UK Biobank and national electronic medical records data offers exciting potential to use such models to risk stratify and predict response to treatments. As a clinician delivering glaucoma care, I understand first-hand the pressure the NHS is under and have witnessed the effect of sight loss on patients. This is why I want to pursue this line of research and help implement changes that improve the health service and patient outcomes.
As this is the main part of your application you should ensure that the information is accurate, succinct and clearly laid out. 1000 words are available for this section if needed.
Please also describe the impact this fellowship will have on your career, noting a fellowship should provide you with a step change in your career trajectory

inclusive_design.text: I will complete the Introduction to Public-Patient Involvement and Introduction to Equality, Diversity and Inclusion in Ophthalmic Research courses (available at no cost). This prepares me to run a patient focus group to inform the doctoral application development. A previous focus group revealed that patients strongly support the use of genetic testing to enable early diagnosis and predict best treatment. I will build on this to garner public opinion on my implementation proposal. Recruitment will be supported by the PPI team at the NIHR Moorfields Biomedical Research Centre and will aim to recruit the most diverse group possible.

research_support.text:
Careful consideration should be given to the choice of primary academic supervisor and a clear justification for the individual proposed should be provided in the application.
i) the numbers of Masters students currently supervised and successfully supervised to completion
ii) Record of conversion of Masters to PhD students
iii) the number of PhD students currently supervised and successfully supervised to completion of PhD
iv) experience in the research area of interest/ relevant methodologies
v) ease of maintaining contact with the supervisor/ availability of the supervisor
vi) suitability for continuation as the PhD supervisor (if the award is successful) or ability of the supervisor to help find the best PhD supervision
The primary doctoral level supervisor must be based at the proposed academic organisation.
My proposed primary supervisor is Professor Prof Khawaja Khawaja, Professor of Ophthalmology at UCL where he leads an internationally reputed data science research group, and Honorary Consultant Ophthalmologist at Moorfields Eye Hospital where he specialises in the care of adults with glaucoma.
Prof Khawaja’s primary research interest is in the genetic and environmental causes of glaucoma, and his group aims to translate discoveries into clinical prediction tools to enable personalised glaucoma care. His ultimate research aim is to combine polygenic models with machine learning image algorithms to develop predictive clinical tools for glaucoma management, enabling targeted population screening and stratified, individualised care in clinics. He is currently UKRI Future Leaders Fellow and has been awarded one of the 2022 Lister Institute Research Prizes to support his research programme.
He is a recognised leading investigator in many aspects of data-driven research, including real-world outcomes, exploratory big data studies and statistical methods. He leads an internationally reputed common eye diseases complex genetics programme and was first author and primary analyst for the largest genome-wide association study (GWAS) of intraocular pressure to date, published in Nature Genetics. This was described as ‘landmark’ due to the leap forward in glaucoma genetics understanding and generated much media interest (Altmetric 211). The 112 genetic loci identified predicted a substantial proportion of people with glaucoma in two independent studies, prompting current research examining the effectiveness of population screening targeted to people at high genetic risk. He also identified biological pathways which point to pathology in parts of the eye previously not thought to be important in glaucoma, opening up the rationale for new treatments (which are already being developed) and helping to sub-categorise glaucoma and tailor treatments accordingly. Prof Khawaja was also joint first author for a landmark GWAS for primary open-angle glaucoma (Nature Communications, 2021) and refractive error (Nature Genetics, 2020). A recent major impact from his research has been the direct to consumer genotyping company, 23andMe, starting to report a polygenic risk score for glaucoma, and suggesting caffeine reduction to reduce risk. His extensive knowledge in the subject matter and the relevant methodologies will be invaluable for supporting the development of my doctoral research plan.
He is also leading the world’s largest data resources that combine clinical outcome and genomic data, including leading a large-scale linkage of national ophthalmic data with UK Biobank which has recently commenced. This is anticipated to be the basis of my Masters and PhD project and he will provide access to this.
Prof Khawaja leads a 3-hour ocular informatics bi-weekly meeting at the UCL Institute of Ophthalmology; researchers within and external to his UCL group present their analyses and receive constructive feedback from the broad expertise that attends (including epidemiology, statistics, computer science, mathematics, genomics). He also hosts journal clubs and external speakers. The group meetings are highly interactive, encourage open discussion of ideas and have a strong track record for high quality research outputs as a result. I will attend these meetings and receive feedback on my research from this broad range of experts. Prof Khawaja will be available for regular 1-1 meetings in addition to this.
Prof Khawaja is an accomplished educator, supervisor and mentor with a successful track record of research supervision; having successfully supervised 2 PhD students and 4 Masters students to completion. He currently supervises 3 PhD students. He is Director of the European Society of Ophthalmology Leadership Development Programme, leading a multi-module programme to enhance leadership skills of emerging ophthalmology leaders from UK/Europe nominated by their national societies. He was also Director of the Academy for Research Training, European School for Advanced Studies in Ophthalmology; he developed and led a multi-module research skills training programme for early-career researchers across Europe. He lectures for the UCL Clinical Ophthalmology MSc and Bioscience Entrepreneurship MSc on Genome-Wide Association Studies, Mendelian Randomisation, Glaucoma Epidemiology and Genetics, Statistical Pitfalls in Ophthalmology and Big Data Research in Ophthalmology (last feedback received: 5/5 from all students across all domains). He has also developed an online interactive glaucoma epidemiology module for the Advanced Clinical Optometry MSc. Prof Khawaja was Glaucoma Service Training Director at Moorfields Eye Hospital between 2017-2021, responsible for training of residents and fellows and leading the UCL/Moorfields weekly Glaucoma Academic Half-day Meeting. He organised the interactive programme including video-based surgical learning, express-format journal club, wet labs and renowned guest speakers. The teaching was highly regarded and fed back as the "best specialist glaucoma teaching nationally". My career development will benefit greatly from his established mentorship skills in both research and clinical contexts.
Prof Khawaja is an esteemed researcher with international acclaim. He holds several research leadership positions, including elected President of the European Eye Epidemiology (E3) Consortium and Chair of both the UK Glaucoma Genetics Consortium and the UK Glaucoma Real-World Data Consortium. He established and leads the successful multidisciplinary international Modifiable Risk Factors for Glaucoma Collaboration, and has a prominent role in the International Glaucoma Genetics Consortium. He is Associate Editor for the journal Eye (Nature Publishing) and is considered a leader in this field, frequently invited as a keynote lecturer nationally and internationally (e.g. 37 invited international lectures in last 3 years). He is co-chair of the Moorfields-UCL NIHR Biomedical Research Centre; and was a key member of the grant writing group for the £20M award. He has authored >150 peer-reviewed publications (Citations: 8136, h-index: 45, i10-index: 101) and has won numerous other prizes and awards in his career, including a Bronze National Clinical Excellence Award and a Secretariat Award and Senior Achievement Award from the American Academy of Ophthalmology. Prof Khawaja has been peer-voted in top 100 thought leaders in ophthalmology globally twice for The Ophthalmologist publication. Early in the pandemic, he established and chaired a regular global webinar with international guests and expert panellists; there have been >600 unique participants from UK & around the world. His reputed expertise in the field coupled with his established track-record as a mentor makes him the ideal PhD supervisor if this award is successful.

My proposed primary supervisor is Professor Prof Khawaja Khawaja, Professor of Ophthalmology at UCL where he leads an internationally reputed data science research group, and Honorary Consultant Ophthalmologist at Moorfields Eye Hospital where he specialises in the care of adults with glaucoma.
Prof Khawaja’s primary research interest is in the genetic and environmental causes of glaucoma, and his group aims to translate discoveries into clinical prediction tools to enable personalised glaucoma care. His ultimate research aim is to combine polygenic models with machine learning image algorithms to develop predictive clinical tools for glaucoma management, enabling targeted population screening and stratified, individualised care in clinics. He is currently UKRI Future Leaders Fellow and has been awarded one of the 2022 Lister Institute Research Prizes to support his research programme.
He is a recognised leading investigator in many aspects of data-driven research, including real-world outcomes, exploratory big data studies and statistical methods. He leads an internationally reputed common eye diseases complex genetics programme and was first author and primary analyst for the largest genome-wide association study (GWAS) of intraocular pressure to date, published in Nature Genetics. This was described as ‘landmark’ due to the leap forward in glaucoma genetics understanding and generated much media interest (Altmetric 211). The 112 genetic loci identified predicted a substantial proportion of people with glaucoma in two independent studies, prompting current research examining the effectiveness of population screening targeted to people at high genetic risk. He also identified biological pathways which point to pathology in parts of the eye previously not thought to be important in glaucoma, opening up the rationale for new treatments (which are already being developed) and helping to sub-categorise glaucoma and tailor treatments accordingly. Prof Khawaja was also joint first author for a landmark GWAS for primary open-angle glaucoma (Nature Communications, 2021) and refractive error (Nature Genetics, 2020). A recent major impact from his research has been the direct to consumer genotyping company, 23andMe, starting to report a polygenic risk score for glaucoma, and suggesting caffeine reduction to reduce risk. His extensive knowledge in the subject matter and the relevant methodologies will be invaluable for supporting the development of my doctoral research plan.
He is also leading the world’s largest data resources that combine clinical outcome and genomic data, including leading a large-scale linkage of national ophthalmic data with UK Biobank which has recently commenced. This is anticipated to be the basis of my Masters and PhD project and he will provide access to this.
Prof Khawaja leads a 3-hour ocular informatics bi-weekly meeting at the UCL Institute of Ophthalmology; researchers within and external to his UCL group present their analyses and receive constructive feedback from the broad expertise that attends (including epidemiology, statistics, computer science, mathematics, genomics). He also hosts journal clubs and external speakers. The group meetings are highly interactive, encourage open discussion of ideas and have a strong track record for high quality research outputs as a result. I will attend these meetings and receive feedback on my research from this broad range of experts. Prof Khawaja will be available for regular 1-1 meetings in addition to this.

This statement is completed by the Heads of Departments of the named Host and Partner organisations and should detail how these organisations are going to support the applicant, in partnership, to successfully complete the PCAF (if awarded).
This statement should be tailored specifically to the applicant, their research and training needs, and include how the organisations intend to support the applicant to develop their research career in the long-term.
NIHR see the responsibility for training the next generation of research leaders as a joint enterprise with host organisations. Therefore, information should also be provided on the organisations’ track records of supporting early career researchers, such as evidence of bridging or other support provided to fellows upon completion of an award.
In addition, the statement should also describe the host organisation’s approach to creating and maintaining an inclusive and supportive research culture for all. The statement should provide evidence of how the organisation values and supports equality, diversity and inclusion as well as acknowledging the organisation’s responsibilities with respect to research integrity. Statements may wish to refer to the principles and best practice outlined within relevant Charters and Concordats in these areas, such as the Researcher Development Concordat and Advance HE’s Equality Charters. It should be noted that being a signatory to Concordats or holding bronze/silver status from the Equality Charters isn’t a requirement of funding and evidence can be provided through other means.
Wing Shing Low has established himself as an exceptional clinician in a range of ophthalmic sub-specialties in the 4 years he has worked in Moorfields Eye Hospital. Having undergone extensive clinical training, he is now a senior optometrist responsible for specialised care with a high degree of clinical autonomy, as well as supervising staff and trainees in multiple disciplines across the glaucoma and contact lens services. In addition to delivering teaching and performing audits, he has made significant contributions to two important research projects within the Trust and demonstrated his potential to become an excellent researcher. Moorfields Eye Hospital and UCL would like to offer our full support in continue to his development as a research scientist & clinician.
We commit to providing 0.9 WTE of protected time for him to develop his research skills. He will be supported by UCL through the Health Data Science MSc, where he will have access to formal data methods training and the collaborative opportunities within the Institute of Health Informatics. Further, he will collaborate with the Ocular Informatics Group - led by the proposed primary supervisor Prof Anthony Khawaja – to gain relevant research experience in this area. The Group is based in the Moorfields Biomedical Research Centre (BRC) - a joint venture between Moorfields and UCL– where he would benefit from the facilities, research infrastructure and collaborative opportunities available there.
Moorfields will also support Wing’s training towards autonomous delivery of YAG laser capsulotomy and glaucoma laser procedures. Selective Laser Trabeculoplasty is widely used in the management of glaucoma worldwide however there remains an urgent need for further research to expand the evidence base and optimise treatment. He will be allocated an experienced laser practitioner as his clinical supervisor and a dedicated laser list to facilitate training according to the Trust training protocol. Once complete he will run a mixed laser list to maintain his existing and new laser skills and this will be maintained throughout the award.
We also commit to securing Wing’s position in Moorfields following the completion of this award and will continue to support his future research development. The optometry department currently employs numerous practitioner academics with a varying mix of research and clinical activity. It is expected that a suitable contractual arrangement would be found to meet his circumstances.
Moorfields and UCL have a strong track record of collaborating to develop early career researchers.. UCL is a signatory of the Concordat to Support the Career Development of Researchers. The quality of its support systems has been recognised through the Vitae HR Excellence in Research Award in 2022. The UCL Institute of Ophthalmology have hosted many post-doctoral research fellows from a range of independent funders including the NIHR, UKRI and the Wellcome Trust. The mentorship and networking opportunities provided through these fellowships have produced numerous research leaders in their fields. Many of them now hold substantive posts within Moorfields and the BRC who will be available to provide advice and guidance for Wing’s career development. Moorfields Eye Charity has several funding programmes designed to support early career researchers, including the 12 months Springboard Award and the Advanced Clinical Academic Fellowship co-funded with the NIHR. Wing will have access to the expertise and advice from a dedicated team at the UCL Institute of Ophthalmology to provide application advice and administrative support for these funding schemes.
UCL considers maintaining the highest standards of research integrity a joint responsibility between UCL and everyone involved in research. The UCL Code of Conduct for Research reflects the commitments set out by the Concordant to Support Research Integrity and UCL is committed to maintain a supportive environment for researchers to adhere to these principles. Diversity in research is also fundamental to research integrity and clear strategies to promote equality, diversity and inclusion (EDI) are embedded in all aspects of research activity at Moorfields and UCL. A dedicated team of co-ordinators and associates continually monitors EDI issues across all aspects of research including funding, recruitment and participation in research at both institutions. They are also tasked with delivering and implementing solutions to address any inequality issues identified and ensure practice is aligned with principles set out by the relevant bodies. UCL’s commitment to gender and racial equality is recognised through the Athena Swan Silver Award and the Race Equality Charter Bronze Award.
Awaiting information from UCL about conversion rate of Masters to PhD and progression of practitioner academics along the ICA Pathway

Dr Dun Jack Fu will be the proposed second supervisor, who is an NIHR Academic Clinical lecturer at the UCL Institute of Ophthalmology and Honorary Clinical Researcher at Moorfields Eye Hospital.
Dr. Fu is trained in molecular biochemistry, high-throughput screening and bioinformatics through his PhD research as a Wellcome Trust Doctoral Fellow at the Centre for Dermatology and Genetic Medicine. He subsequently completed medical training at University of Oxford and is undertaking ophthalmology specialty training at Moorfields Eye Hospital. His current post-doctoral research focuses on the development of clinical prediction modelling with large, multimodal datasets using both traditional prediction modelling approaches and modern machine learning techniques. This expertise is highly pertinent to my research area of interest and will greatly benefit my training. His experience in conducting data-intensive research makes him well placed to support my doctoral research plan development.

He has designed and written content for 2 additional UCL courses – Telemedicine for Ophthalmology Postgraduate Certificate and Data Science for Clinicians. His insights in developing digital tools for health will be beneficial in shaping my doctoral research output.

My proposed primary supervisor is Professor Prof Khawaja Khawaja, Professor of Ophthalmology at UCL where he leads an internationally reputed data science research group, and Honorary Consultant Ophthalmologist at Moorfields Eye Hospital where he specialises in the care of adults with glaucoma.
Prof Khawaja’s primary research interest is in the genetic and environmental causes of glaucoma, and his group aims to translate discoveries into clinical prediction tools to enable personalised glaucoma care. His ultimate research aim is to combine polygenic models with machine learning image algorithms to develop predictive clinical tools for glaucoma management, enabling targeted population screening and stratified, individualised care in clinics. He is currently UKRI Future Leaders Fellow and has been awarded one of the 2022 Lister Institute Research Prizes to support his research programme.
He is a recognised leading investigator in many aspects of data-driven research, including real-world outcomes, exploratory big data studies and statistical methods. He leads an internationally reputed common eye diseases complex genetics programme and was first author and primary analyst for the largest genome-wide association study (GWAS) of intraocular pressure to date, published in Nature Genetics. This was described as ‘landmark’ due to the leap forward in glaucoma genetics understanding and generated much media interest (Altmetric 211). The 112 genetic loci identified predicted a substantial proportion of people with glaucoma in two independent studies, prompting current research examining the effectiveness of population screening targeted to people at high genetic risk. He also identified biological pathways which point to pathology in parts of the eye previously not thought to be important in glaucoma, opening up the rationale for new treatments (which are already being developed) and helping to sub-categorise glaucoma and tailor treatments accordingly. This statement is completed by the Heads of Departments of the named Host and Partner organisations and should detail how these organisations are going to support the applicant, in partnership, to successfully complete the PCAF (if awarded).
This statement should be tailored specifically to the applicant, their research and training needs, and include how the organisations intend to support the applicant to develop their research career in the long-term.
NIHR see the responsibility for training the next generation of research leaders as a joint enterprise with host organisations. Therefore, information should also be provided on the organisations’ track records of supporting early career researchers, such as evidence of bridging or other support provided to fellows upon completion of an award.
In addition, the statement should also describe the host organisation’s approach to creating and maintaining an inclusive and supportive research culture for all. The statement should provide evidence of how the organisation values and supports equality, diversity and inclusion as well as acknowledging the organisation’s responsibilities with respect to research integrity. Statements may wish to refer to the principles and best practice outlined within relevant Charters and Concordats in these areas, such as the Researcher Development Concordat and Advance HE’s Equality Charters. It should be noted that being a signatory to Concordats or holding bronze/silver status from the Equality Charters isn’t a requirement of funding and evidence can be provided through other means.
Wing Shing Low has established himself as an exceptional clinician in a range of ophthalmic sub-specialties in the 4 years he has worked in Moorfields Eye Hospital. Having undergone extensive clinical training, he is now a senior optometrist responsible for specialised care with a high degree of clinical autonomy, as well as supervising staff and trainees in multiple disciplines across the glaucoma and contact lens services. In addition to delivering teaching and performing audits, he has made significant contributions to two important research projects within the Trust and demonstrated his potential to become an excellent researcher. Moorfields Eye Hospital and UCL would like to offer our full support in continue to his development as a research scientist & clinician.
We commit to providing 0.9 WTE of protected time for him to develop his research skills. He will be supported by UCL through the Health Data Science MSc, where he will have access to formal data methods training and the collaborative opportunities within the Institute of Health Informatics. Further, he will collaborate with the Ocular Informatics Group - led by the proposed primary supervisor Prof Anthony Khawaja – to gain relevant research experience in this area. The Group is based in the Moorfields Biomedical Research Centre (BRC) - a joint venture between Moorfields and UCL– where he would benefit from the facilities, research infrastructure and collaborative opportunities available there.
Moorfields will also support Wing’s training towards autonomous delivery of YAG laser capsulotomy and glaucoma laser procedures. Selective Laser Trabeculoplasty is widely used in the management of glaucoma worldwide however there remains an urgent need for further research to expand the evidence base and optimise treatment. He will be allocated an experienced laser practitioner as his clinical supervisor and a dedicated laser list to facilitate training according to the Trust training protocol. Once complete he will run a mixed laser list to maintain his existing and new laser skills and this will be maintained throughout the award.
We also commit to securing Wing’s position in Moorfields following the completion of this award and will continue to support his future research development. The optometry department currently employs numerous practitioner academics with a varying mix of research and clinical activity. It is expected that a suitable contractual arrangement would be found to meet his circumstances.
Moorfields and UCL have a strong track record of collaborating to develop early career researchers.. UCL is a signatory of the Concordat to Support the Career Development of Researchers. The quality of its support systems has been recognised through the Vitae HR Excellence in Research Award in 2022. The UCL Institute of Ophthalmology have hosted many post-doctoral research fellows from a range of independent funders including the NIHR, UKRI and the Wellcome Trust. The mentorship and networking opportunities provided through these fellowships have produced numerous research leaders in their fields. Many of them now hold substantive posts within Moorfields and the BRC who will be available to provide advice and guidance for Wing’s career development. Moorfields Eye Charity has several funding programmes designed to support early career researchers, including the 12 months Springboard Award and the Advanced Clinical Academic Fellowship co-funded with the NIHR. Wing will have access to the expertise and advice from a dedicated team at the UCL Institute of Ophthalmology to provide application advice and administrative support for these funding schemes.
UCL considers maintaining the highest standards of research integrity a joint responsibility between UCL and everyone involved in research. The UCL Code of Conduct for Research reflects the commitments set out by the Concordant to Support Research Integrity and UCL is committed to maintain a supportive environment for researchers to adhere to these principles. Diversity in research is also fundamental to research integrity and clear strategies to promote equality, diversity and inclusion (EDI) are embedded in all aspects of research activity at Moorfields and UCL. A dedicated team of co-ordinators and associates continually monitors EDI issues across all aspects of research including funding, recruitment and participation in research at both institutions. They are also tasked with delivering and implementing solutions to address any inequality issues identified and ensure practice is aligned with principles set out by the relevant bodies. UCL’s commitment to gender and racial equality is recognised through the Athena Swan Silver Award and the Race Equality Charter Bronze Award.
Awaiting information from UCL about conversion rate of Masters to PhD and progression of practitioner academics along the ICA Pathway

Many of them now hold substantive posts within Moorfields and the BRC who will be available to provide advice and guidance for Wing’s career development. Moorfields Eye Charity has several funding programmes designed to support early career researchers, including the 12 months Springboard Award and the Advanced Clinical Academic Fellowship co-funded with the NIHR. Wing will have access to the expertise and advice from a dedicated team at the UCL Institute of Ophthalmology to provide application advice and administrative support for these funding schemes.
UCL considers maintaining the highest standards of research integrity a joint responsibility between UCL and everyone involved in research. The UCL Code of Conduct for Research reflects the commitments set out by the Concordant to Support Research Integrity and UCL is committed to maintain a supportive environment for researchers to adhere to these principles. Diversity in research is also fundamental to research integrity and clear strategies to promote equality, diversity and inclusion (EDI) are embedded in all aspects of research activity at Moorfields and UCL. A dedicated team of co-ordinators and associates continually monitors EDI issues across all aspects of research including funding, recruitment and participation in research at both institutions. They are also tasked with delivering and implementing solutions to address any inequality issues identified and ensure practice is aligned with principles set out by the relevant bodies. UCL’s commitment to gender and racial equality is recognised through the Athena Swan Silver Award and the Race Equality Charter Bronze Award.

This statement is completed by the Heads of Departments of the named Host and Partner organisations and should detail how these organisations are going to support the applicant, in partnership, to successfully complete the PCAF (if awarded).
This statement should be tailored specifically to the applicant, their research and training needs, and include how the organisations intend to support the applicant to develop their research career in the long-term.
NIHR see the responsibility for training the next generation of research leaders as a joint enterprise with host organisations. Therefore, information should also be provided on the organisations’ track records of supporting early career researchers, such as evidence of bridging or other support provided to fellows upon completion of an award.
In addition, the statement should also describe the host organisation’s approach to creating and maintaining an inclusive and supportive research culture for all. The statement should provide evidence of how the organisation values and supports equality, diversity and inclusion as well as acknowledging the organisation’s responsibilities with respect to research integrity. Statements may wish to refer to the principles and best practice outlined within relevant Charters and Concordats in these areas, such as the Researcher Development Concordat and Advance HE’s Equality Charters. It should be noted that being a signatory to Concordats or holding bronze/silver status from the Equality Charters isn’t a requirement of funding and evidence can be provided through other means.
Wing Shing Low has established himself as an exceptional clinician in a range of ophthalmic sub-specialties in the 4 years he has worked in Moorfields Eye Hospital. Having undergone extensive clinical training, he is now a senior optometrist responsible for specialised care with a high degree of clinical autonomy, as well as supervising staff and trainees in multiple disciplines across the glaucoma and contact lens services. In addition to delivering teaching and performing audits, he has made significant contributions to two important research projects within the Trust and demonstrated his potential to become an excellent researcher. Moorfields Eye Hospital and UCL would like to offer our full support in continue to his development as a research scientist & clinician.
We commit to providing 0.9 WTE of protected time for him to develop his research skills. He will be supported by UCL through the Health Data Science MSc, where he will have access to formal data methods training and the collaborative opportunities within the Institute of Health Informatics. Further, he will collaborate with the Ocular Informatics Group - led by the proposed primary supervisor Prof Anthony Khawaja – to gain relevant research experience in this area. The Group is based in the Moorfields Biomedical Research Centre (BRC) - a joint venture between Moorfields and UCL– where he would benefit from the facilities, research infrastructure and collaborative opportunities available there.
Moorfields will also support Wing’s training towards autonomous delivery of YAG laser capsulotomy and glaucoma laser procedures. Selective Laser Trabeculoplasty is widely used in the management of glaucoma worldwide however there remains an urgent need for further research to expand the evidence base and optimise treatment. He will be allocated an experienced laser practitioner as his clinical supervisor and a dedicated laser list to facilitate training according to the Trust training protocol. Once complete he will run a mixed laser list to maintain his existing and new laser skills and this will be maintained throughout the award.
We also commit to securing Wing’s position in Moorfields following the completion of this award and will continue to support his future research development. The optometry department currently employs numerous practitioner academics with a varying mix of research and clinical activity. It is expected that a suitable contractual arrangement would be found to meet his circumstances.

overall_ppi.text: I will complete the Introduction to Public-Patient Involvement and Introduction to Equality, Diversity and Inclusion in Ophthalmic Research courses (available at no cost). This prepares me to run a patient focus group to inform the doctoral application development. A previous focus group revealed that patients strongly support the use of genetic testing to enable early diagnosis and predict best treatment. I will build on this to garner public opinion on my implementation proposal. Recruitment will be supported by the PPI team at the NIHR Moorfields Biomedical Research Centre and will aim to recruit the most diverse group possible.
